Slide 22
↑ Should fix
Sources: X4
INSERT in the Review section (after the Math_Trap slides, before Slide 22): A Shout Out Loud slide with four choral prompts displayed one at a time: (1) 'A letter representing a changing quantity is called a…' → VARIABLE; (2) 'A number that stays the same is called a…' → CONSTANT; (3) 'A mathematical expression using letters and numbers is called an…' → ALGEBRAIC FORM; (4) '3 × n written without the × sign is…' → 3n. This fires at the vocabulary fluency milestone and matches the blueprint's Shout Out Loud design.
↑ Should fix
Sources: D5, X2, L4
EDIT Slide 22 (Module Summary): (1) Differentiate the three bullet points by size or colour—make the first bullet (definition of variable) the largest/boldest anchor, with the other two as supporting points at smaller weight. (2) Add a forward-pointer sentence at the bottom: 'In the next lesson, we will explore the parts of an algebraic expression—variables, constants, and coefficients—in more detail.' This creates the handoff to G7C4M2 and closes the knowledge state loop. (3) Add one explicit effort-framing sentence, e.g., 'Thinking carefully about what each letter stands for is exactly what mathematicians do.'
Scorer details (6 annotations, 2 cross-refs)
D5 — Knowledge state management 2/3
Covers slides 2–3, 22
Evidence
Exit state: Slide 22 ('What Did We Learn?') summarises the three key takeaways (unknown quantity → variable → letter; variable represents a number; different unknowns need different letters). This is a functional exit state. However, two gaps reduce the score: (1) The entry state does not explicitly reference what students already know from prior modules — there is no 'In your last lesson you learned X; today we build on that' moment. The number game is a motivational hook, not a prerequisite activation. (2) The handoff to the next module is absent: Slide 22 ends the module with no forward pointer ('In the next lesson, we will look at the parts of an algebraic expression in more detail'). The blueprint's convention_requirement for 'algebraic form vs. algebraic expression' disambiguation (pointing forward to G7C4M2) is not delivered. (3) The module's own LO (writing algebraic form using 3n notation) is not reflected in the exit state — Slide 22 summarises only the variable/unknown concept, not the algebraic form or 3n notation, because those concepts were never taught.
D7 — Trope placement & sequencing 2/4
Covers slides 5, 8–10, 13–15, 19–22
Evidence
The module ends with a summary slide (Slide 22, 'Module Complete!') but there is no Commit-Before-Reveal exit question.
X2 — Layout & visual hierarchy 2/3
Covers slides 8–9, 16, 21–22
Evidence
Slide 22 (summary) stacks three bullet points in a single text block with no size/colour differentiation between the three takeaways — uniform weight makes scanning difficult. Trope icons (Snapshot, Math Vault, Math Trap, Shout Out Loud) are present and identifiable in the top-right corner throughout.
X3 — Visible self-sufficiency 3/6
Covers slides 10, 13–14, 22
Evidence
(4) Slide 22 (Module Complete) carries a summary but no Exit_Question — the teacher has no visible diagnostic moment to close the lesson. The absence of a Math_Trap for M252
Misconception medium
Students believe variables are mysterious unknown numbers to be 'solved for', rather than placeholders that represent any value in a pattern or rule. In this module's context, students see 'n' and immediately try to find its value rather than accepting it as a label for 'any pattern number'. This is the primary target misconception for this module.
Present a completed table (pattern 1→3, 2→6, 3→9) and the expression 3n, then ask: 'What is n?' If the student says 'I need to solve for n' or writes a single number, the misconception is active. The Math_Trap slide should show a student 'solving' 3n = 12 and ask whether this is the right move.
 (the primary target misconception) means the teacher has no visible cue to surface and confront the 'solve for n' error.
L1 — Vocabulary discipline 2/4
Covers slides 8–9, 17, 22
Evidence
Slide 22 summarises both terms. However: (1) 'algebraic form' — a required vocabulary term — is never introduced, defined, or bolded anywhere in the module. (2) The notation '3n' is never introduced or explained; the implicit multiplication convention (3 × n = 3n) is entirely absent from the module. (3) The variable 'n' as a pattern-position label is never introduced; the module uses 'a' and 'b' as example variables but never establishes 'n' as the required notation. (4) The formalization sequence is incomplete: the module moves from informal 'unknown quantity' to the formal term 'variable' but never reaches 'algebraic form' or the notation 3n.
L2 — Age-appropriate language 3/3
Covers slides 3, 8, 14, 22
Evidence
Slide 22 summary uses slightly longer sentences but remains accessible. Technical vocabulary ('variable', 'constant') is introduced with definitions before being used. No assumed prior knowledge of algebraic terminology.
D2 — Difficulty gradation see Slide 3
D4 — Scaffolding arc see Slide 8
Conceptreinforces variable: a letter that represents a number we do not know yet
reinforces
Slide 22 summary: 'A variable is a letter that represents a number we do not know yet.'
strength: explicit
 reinforces different unknown quantities must be represented by different letters
reinforces
Module summary: 'While assigning a variable, we should remember that different unknowns are represented by different letters (variables).'
strength: explicit
After completing this module as reviewed, students can distinguish known from unknown quantities in static scenarios and assign letters to unknowns. However, they cannot identify changing vs. fixed quantities in a growing pattern, write algebraic form using 3n notation, or explain why algebraic form is useful. The module delivers approximately 25% of the approved LO—a genuine prerequisite skill but not the LO itself.
Mastery Assessment 25% coverage
Coverage 25%
The module delivers approximately 25% of the approved LO. It successfully introduces the concept that letters can represent quantities (variable as label) and the rule that different unknowns need different letters — these are genuine, well-taught sub-skills. However, the three core deliverables of the approved LO are entirely absent: (1) identifying fixed vs. changing quantities in a growing pattern, (2) writing the algebraic form using the notation 3n, and (3) understanding 'algebraic form' as a concept. The module also fails to introduce two of the three required vocabulary terms ('algebraic form' is absent; 'constant' is introduced informally but not in a Math_Vault) and both required notations ('n' as pattern-position label and '3n' as implicit multiplication are absent). Three hard gates fail (G1: LO misaligned, G2: no CPA trace for the required LO, G5: knowledge gaps — algebraic form, 3n, and n as pattern label are never taught), confirming the module is gate-blocked. The 25% estimate reflects the genuine partial coverage of the variable-as-label sub-skill, which is a real prerequisite for the LO even if it is not the LO itself.
What students CAN do after this module
After completing this module as reviewed, students can fluently: (1) distinguish a known quantity (constant) from an unknown quantity (variable) in a static concrete scenario (fruit sacks, pencil boxes); (2) assign a letter to represent a single unknown quantity; (3) state that different unknown quantities require different letters; (4) recall the definition of 'variable' as 'a letter that represents a number we do not know yet.' These are partial fluencies relative to the approved LO.
Fluency gaps — what students CANNOT reliably do
Students cannot yet: (1) identify a changing quantity vs. a fixed quantity in a growing pattern — the module never presents a growing pattern; (2) use n as a pattern-position label rather than a mystery unknown to solve for — the module frames variables exclusively as 'numbers we do not know yet', which is the M252
Misconception medium
Students believe variables are mysterious unknown numbers to be 'solved for', rather than placeholders that represent any value in a pattern or rule. In this module's context, students see 'n' and immediately try to find its value rather than accepting it as a label for 'any pattern number'. This is the primary target misconception for this module.
Present a completed table (pattern 1→3, 2→6, 3→9) and the expression 3n, then ask: 'What is n?' If the student says 'I need to solve for n' or writes a single number, the misconception is active. The Math_Trap slide should show a student 'solving' 3n = 12 and ask whether this is the right move.
 misconception framing; (3) write the algebraic form 3n or 1 + 3n — the notation 3n is entirely absent from the module; (4) explain what 'algebraic form' means — the term is never introduced or defined; (5) apply the implicit multiplication convention (3 × n = 3n) — this convention is never taught; (6) distinguish the constant part from the variable part of an algebraic expression derived from a pattern. The module's ceiling is well below the approved LO: students leave knowing how to label a static unknown with a letter, but not how to write an algebraic form for a growing pattern.
Conceptual depth
2/5 — Students understand that a letter can stand for a number they do not know yet (a surface-level, pre-algebraic idea), and that different unknowns need different letters. However, they have no understanding of WHY algebraic form is useful (it describes a rule for any value of n, not just one), no understanding of the variable/constant distinction in a pattern context, and no understanding of the implicit multiplication convention. The module teaches the label function of variables but not the rule-description function, which is the conceptual core of the approved LO.
Transfer readiness
1/5 — Students cannot transfer to a novel growing-pattern context because the pattern-to-expression generalisation was never taught. The only transferable skill is assigning a letter to a static unknown in a new scenario (e.g., 'Budi has some marbles — let m = number of marbles'), which is a pre-algebraic skill below the LO. Students are not ready for G7C4M2 (Anatomy of an Algebraic Expression) because they have not encountered the algebraic form 1 + 3n from which terms, coefficients, and constants are identified.
Mastery path to 100
Students have no opportunity to observe a growing pattern and distinguish fixed from changing quantities — the foundational concrete experience for the approved LO is entirely absent.
Unlocks the entire approved LO — without this concrete foundation, no subsequent gap can be closed. Also directly addresses the LO misalignment (Gate G1) and the missing CPA trace (Gate G2).
Insert a concrete-phase teach sequence using Nyoman's matchstick square pattern (Exploration 4.1, p.127): (1) Progressive Reveal of Figures 1–3 with the question 'What changes? What stays the same?'; (2) a two-column fixed-vs-changing quantity table (orange = fixed, blue = changing); (3) derivation of 3n as the changing quantity and 1 + 3n as the algebraic form. This is the prerequisite for every other gap below.
M252 (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is the highest-severity misconception in scope and is not addressed — the module's current framing of variables as 'numbers we do not know yet' actively primes this misconception rather than countering it.
Closes the primary misconception gap (M252) and reframes the module's core concept correctly, enabling students to accept n as a rule-description label rather than a mystery to solve.
After the growing-pattern teach sequence, insert a Math_Trap slide: show a student character ('Budi') who sees 3n and writes '3n = 12, so n = 4'; use Counterexample Feedback (table: n=1→3, n=2→6, n=3→9, n=4→12) to show that n is a pattern-position label valid for any figure number, not a fixed unknown. Reframe the module's variable definition from 'a number we do not know yet' to 'a letter representing a changing quantity in a pattern.'
M251 precursor (combining 1 + 3n into 4n) is unaddressed — students who derive 1 + 3n will immediately face this error and have no defence against it.
Closes the M251 precursor gap and reinforces the constant/variable distinction at the expression level, preparing students for G7C4M2's full treatment of terms and coefficients.
After deriving 1 + 3n, insert a second Math_Trap: show a student ('Sari') writing '1 + 3n = 4n'; use Counterexample Feedback (substitute n=2: 1+6=7 ≠ 4×2=8) to show the constant and variable part cannot be combined. Colour-code: 1 in orange (constant), 3n in blue (variable part).
The notation 3n (implicit multiplication convention) is never introduced — students cannot write or interpret the algebraic form without this convention.
Unlocks the ability to write and read algebraic form — without this, students cannot produce or interpret 3n or 1 + 3n, making the LO unachievable.
Add a Math_Vault slide showing '3 × n = 3n' side by side with explicit statement: 'In algebra, we drop the × sign when a number multiplies a letter.' Colour-code the multiplier and the variable. Reinforce in every worked example and in the Exit_Question reveal.
The term 'algebraic form' is never introduced or defined — one of the three required vocabulary terms is entirely absent, and the module's LO explicitly requires students to 'write the algebraic form.'
Closes the vocabulary gap for 'algebraic form' and gives students the language to describe what they have derived, enabling the Shout_Out_Loud and Exit_Question moments to fire at the correct conceptual level.
Extend the Math_Vault slide (currently Slide 9, which defines only 'variable') to cover all three required terms: variable (a letter representing a changing quantity), constant (a number that stays fixed), and algebraic form (a mathematical expression using letters and numbers to describe a rule). Use the labelled expression 1 + 3n as the anchor image.
Students have no independent transfer task — the only practice is teacher-narrated worked examples and binary identification tasks (which box is closed?). There is no moment where students write an algebraic form for a new growing pattern without scaffolding.
Closes the transfer gap and provides the diagnostic evidence that students have achieved the approved LO. Also delivers the required Exit_Question trope and completes the scaffolding arc (fully worked → guided → independent).
Insert an Exit_Question slide (Commit-Before-Reveal) presenting a new growing tile pattern (e.g., 2 fixed corner tiles + 5 tiles per figure); ask students to independently write: (1) the fixed quantity, (2) the changing quantity, (3) the algebraic form for Figure n. Reveal: constant = 2, variable part = 5n, algebraic form = 2 + 5n. Verify with n=1 and n=2.
The module's LO slide (Slide 2) frames the module around 'unknown quantities to be solved for' — the opposite of the approved LO's pattern-position framing — and omits 'algebraic form', 'constant', and '3n' entirely.
Aligns the module's stated LO with the approved curriculum LO, sets correct expectations for students and teachers, and removes the M252-priming framing from the module's entry point.
Rewrite Slide 2 to state the approved LO: '(1) Identify a changing quantity in a growing pattern and represent it using a letter (variable); (2) Distinguish fixed quantities (constants) from changing quantities (variables); (3) Write the algebraic form for a pattern using the notation 3n.' Add a prerequisite line: 'You already know: growing patterns, multiplication, and how to describe a rule in words.'
The module uses a generic number-game hook (Slides 3–5) with no Indonesian cultural grounding, and does not use the curriculum-mandated textbook context (Nyoman's matchstick pattern, Exploration 4.1, p.127) as the primary teaching context.
Closes the curriculum alignment gap (KM.CP.FD.ALJ.1, CCSS 6.EE.A.2a) and the geographic viability gap, grounding the module in the approved Indonesian cultural context.
Replace the number-game hook with Nyoman's matchstick square pattern as the primary concrete context (or restructure the hook to lead into the matchstick pattern). Retain the Candra fruit context as a secondary example for the variable-as-label sub-skill. This satisfies Kurikulum Merdeka alignment (KM.CP.FD.ALJ.1) and the textbook reference requirement.
Reframe the module's LO to match the curriculum specification (pattern-position framing, not mystery-unknown framing). Insert a concrete-phase teach sequence using Nyoman's matchstick pattern (Exploration 4.1, p.127) with a fixed-vs-changing table, followed by a function machine pictorial bridge and abstract derivation of 1 + 3n. Add Math_Trap slides for M252 (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) and M251 precursor (combining 1 + 3n into 4n). Introduce all three required vocabulary terms (variable, constant, algebraic form) and the notation 3n in a Math_Vault. These changes address the three failed gates and unlock the approved LO.
Path to 100 — Priority Actions ⊘ Must fix 3 · ↑ Should fix 27 · 2 mastery gaps
⊘ Must fix G1 Singular or clustered LO
Reframe the entire module LO to match the curriculum specification: 'Identify a changing quantity in a growing pattern and represent it using a letter (variable), distinguishing it from fixed quantities (constants), and write the algebraic form using the notation 3n.' Replace slide 2's learning objectives (which frame variables as 'unknown quantities to be solved for') with objectives grounded in pattern generalisation: (1) identify fixed vs. changing quantities in a growing pattern, (2) use n as a label for the pattern position, (3) write the algebraic form 3n and 1 + 3n. The current LO framing actively primes M252 — the primary misconception this module must counter.
Module has one specific, testable LO — or a tight cluster of immediately connected LOs.
⊘ Must fix G2 CPA trace exists
Implement the blueprint CPA sequence: (1) Concrete — Nyoman's matchstick square pattern (Exploration 4.1, p.127): show figures 1–3 with progressive reveal, complete the fixed-vs-changing table with colour-coding (orange = constant 1, blue = changing quantity 3n). (2) Pictorial bridge — Function Machine (VIR 203): input = pattern number n, rule = ×3, output = matchsticks added; show three passes (n=1→3, n=2→6, n=3→9). (3) Abstract — derive 1 + 3n, introduce the Math_Vault definitions of variable, constant, and algebraic form, and show '3 × n = 3n' side by side. This sequence is fully specified in the ideal blueprint at positions 3–9.
At least one concrete → pictorial → abstract sequence is traceable through the module.
⊘ Must fix G5 No knowledge gaps
Introduce all three required vocabulary terms (algebraic form, variable, constant) with their pattern-grounded definitions before any practice activity. Specifically: (1) Add the fixed-vs-changing table activity (blueprint position 4) before the Math_Vault slide; (2) Rewrite the Math_Vault (slide 9) to define all three terms with colour-coded examples from the matchstick pattern; (3) Add a dedicated slide introducing '3n = 3 × n' with the implicit multiplication convention shown side-by-side (blueprint position 6); (4) Add the algebraic form 1 + 3n derivation (blueprint position 8) before any practice. Remove or relocate the 'Math Twist' slides (10, 13, 14) which introduce the area formula L × W — this content is out of scope for this module and introduces a knowledge gap by using variables in a formula context before the pattern-position framing is established.
Every concept used is either prerequisite-activated, taught here, or flagged as first encounter.
+3
⊘ Must fix X1 X · 2/5 +inserthigh confidence
Insert a matchstick-pattern progressive reveal sequence (VIR 161) after slide 9, showing Figure 1 (4 matchsticks), Figure 2 (7 matchsticks), Figure 3 (10 matchsticks) with a two-column fixed-vs-changing table.
+3
⊘ Must fix X3 X · 3/6 +inserthigh confidence
Insert: (a) A Math_Vault slide after the matchstick pattern sequence explicitly showing '3 × n = 3n' with the instruction 'Tell students: In algebra, we drop the × sign when a number multiplies a letter' — making the teaching action visible.

↑ Should fix P2 P · 2/4 +inserthigh confidence
Insert a concrete-phase teach sequence (3 slides minimum) after slide 10 using Nyoman's matchstick square pattern from Exploration 4.1 (p.127): (1) Progressive Reveal of figures 1–3 with the question 'What changes? What stays the same?'; (2) A two-column fixed-vs-changing quantity table (colour-coded: fixed = orange, changing = blue); (3) Derivation of 3n as the changing quantity and 1 + 3n as the algebraic form.

↑ Should fix P5 P · 2/4 +inserthigh confidence
Insert a complete T+G sequence for the algebraic form concept (see P2 recommendation for the Teach slides).

↑ Should fix D1 D · 2/4 ✎edithigh confidence
Slide 5→6: Add a bridging sentence at the bottom of Slide 5 or as a transition beat: 'Now let's see how mathematicians write down quantities they don't know yet — meet Candra.' Slide 9→10: Either remove Slide 10 (the Area = L × W detour is a tangent that breaks the Candra fruit thread) or add a one-sentence setup before it: 'You've actually used variables before — in geometry!' and a one-sentence resolution after it before Slide 11.

↑ Should fix D2 D · 2/4 +inserthigh confidence
After Slide 19 (post-practice consolidation), insert a graduated application sequence: (1) a direct-application slide asking students to assign a variable to a new single unknown (e.g., 'Budi has some marbles in a bag — write a variable for the number of marbles'); (2) a variation slide asking students to write a simple expression for a quantity that changes (e.g., 'Each day Budi adds 3 marbles — write an expression for the total after n days').

Insert two slides after Slide 16 (post-Shout Out Loud): (1) A partially-worked slide: 'Candra now has a bag of oranges and 5 mangoes on a plate.

Revise slide 2 to state the full module LO: 'We will be able to: (1) Identify a changing quantity in a growing pattern and represent it using a letter (variable); (2) Distinguish fixed quantities (constants) from changing quantities (variables); (3) Write the algebraic form for a pattern using the notation 3n.' Add a prerequisite line: 'You already know: growing patterns, multiplication, and how to describe a rule in words.' This aligns the LO slide with the approved module LO and KM.TP.FD.ALJ.1.

◦ Consider P3 P · 2/3 +inserthigh confidence
After the matchstick pattern teach sequence (see P2 recommendation), add at least one additional varied example of algebraic form from a different growing pattern context — e.g., a tile arrangement or a warung-stall scenario (see blueprint slide 16, batik workshop context).

◦ Consider P4 P · 2/3 +inserthigh confidence
After the algebraic form derivation sequence (see P2 recommendation), insert a Math_Trap slide for M252: show a student character (e.g., 'Budi') who sees 3n and writes '3n = 12, so n = 4.' Ask: 'Is Budi right? Is n = 4 the only answer?' Use Counterexample Feedback: show the table (n=1→3, n=2→6, n=3→9, n=4→12) and ask 'Which is the right value of n?' — answer: all of them.

◦ Consider P6 P · 2/3 +inserthigh confidence

Recommended Enhancements (3) — advisory, not verdict-affecting

These are placement suggestions from advisory gates — enrichments that would strengthen the module but do NOT fail it. Each entry targets a specific slide position with a specific trope/stage addition, grounded in the blueprint's applet sandwich and seven-trope conventions.

G3 ·
Exit_Question slide — Slide 22 (final slide, replacing or after the current 'Module Complete' summary)
Why: The Exit_Question is a required trope for this module (per tropes_expected) and serves as the diagnostic check of the module LO — confirming students can identify fixed vs. changing quantities and write an algebraic form for a new growing pattern.
How: Display a new growing tile pattern (e.g., 2 fixed corner tiles + 5 tiles per figure); ask students to commit independently: (1) What is the fixed quantity? (2) What is the changing quantity? (3) Write the algebraic form for Figure n. Then reveal: constant = 2, variable part = 5n, algebraic form = 2 + 5n. Verify with n=1 and n=2.
G3 ·
Math_Trap slide for M252 (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) — After the Math_Vault slide (currently slide 9), as a new slide ~10
Why: M252 is the primary misconception this module must address per curriculum specification. The current Math_Trap (slide 21) addresses a different misconception. M252 must be explicitly surfaced and countered: students see 3n and immediately try to 'solve for n'.
How: Show a student character (e.g., Budi) saying 'I see 3n. I need to find n. So 3n = 12, n = 4.' Ask the class: 'Is Budi right? Is n = 4 the only answer?' Show the pattern table: n=1→3, n=2→6, n=3→9, n=4→12. Ask: 'Which is the right value of n?' Answer: all of them — n is the pattern position label, not a fixed unknown. Correct framing: 'We are not solving for n; we are using n to describe the rule for any figure.'
G3 ·
Math_Vault slide — realigned definition of 'variable' as pattern-position label — Replace or supplement slide 9 (current Math_Vault)
Why: The current Math_Vault defines variable as 'a number we do not know yet' — this is the M252 misconception framing. The required definition for this module is 'a letter representing a changing quantity in a pattern'. The Math_Vault is the formal anchor for vocabulary and must not embed the misconception.
How: Rewrite the Math_Vault to read: 'VARIABLE — a letter that represents a changing quantity. In Nyoman's matchstick pattern, n is the variable because the figure number changes. CONSTANT — a number that stays the same. In this pattern, 1 is the constant because the fixed corner matchstick never changes. ALGEBRAIC FORM — a mathematical expression using letters and numbers to describe a rule.' Colour-code n in blue (changing), 1 in orange (fixed).
Translation Notes (23) — localisation metadata, not scored

The scorers encountered these bilingual term references in the curriculum context and blueprint. They are preserved here as deliverables for a later translation or localisation pass. The native-language fragments were NOT used to score vocabulary coverage — only the primary term was matched against delivered slide content.

Primary Native Lang Slide Type Context
Fixed quantity constant Slide 4 convention Constant '1' in 1 + 3n — fixed quantity from the pattern: Use the fixed-vs-changing table from Exploration 4.1 explicitl
Changing quantity variable part Slide 4 convention Constant '1' in 1 + 3n — fixed quantity from the pattern: Use the fixed-vs-changing table from Exploration 4.1 explicitl
variable variabel Indonesian — vocabulary Core algebraic term; Indonesian form is a direct cognate of the English.
constant konstanta Indonesian — vocabulary A fixed numerical value in an algebraic expression with no variable; Indonesian form is a direct cognate.
algebraic form bentuk aljabar Indonesian — vocabulary A mathematical phrase combining variables, coefficients, and constants; Indonesian uses 'bentuk aljabar' (literally 'alg
coefficient koefisien Indonesian — vocabulary The numerical multiplier of a variable in an algebraic term; Indonesian form is a direct cognate. Note: 'coefficient' is
term suku Indonesian — vocabulary A single component of an algebraic expression separated by + or −; Indonesian uses 'suku' (literally 'part/member'), not
n n Indonesian — notation The letter used as a variable to represent the pattern number; notation is identical in both languages. Indonesian textb
3n 3n Indonesian — notation Shorthand for 3 × n; the multiplication sign is omitted when a number multiplies a variable. Notation is identical in bo
algebraic expression bentuk aljabar Indonesian — vocabulary Synonym for 'algebraic form' used in the chapter; Indonesian uses the same term 'bentuk aljabar' for both. Not the prima
substitution substitusi Indonesian — vocabulary Replacing a variable with a specific numerical value to evaluate an expression; Indonesian form is a direct cognate. Out
like terms suku sejenis Indonesian — vocabulary Terms with identical variable parts; Indonesian uses 'suku sejenis' (literally 'terms of the same type'). Out of scope f
unlike terms suku tidak sejenis Indonesian — vocabulary Terms with different variable parts; Indonesian uses 'suku tidak sejenis'. Out of scope for this module but carried forw
collect like terms menyederhanakan suku sejenis Indonesian — convention The process of combining like terms to simplify an expression; Indonesian textbooks typically use 'menyederhanakan' (sim
distributive property sifat distributif Indonesian — vocabulary The property that a(b + c) = ab + ac; Indonesian uses 'sifat distributif'. Out of scope for this module but carried forw
commutative property sifat komutatif Indonesian — vocabulary The property that a + b = b + a; Indonesian uses 'sifat komutatif'. Out of scope for this module but carried forward.
associative property sifat asosiatif Indonesian — vocabulary The property that (a + b) + c = a + (b + c); Indonesian uses 'sifat asosiatif'. Out of scope for this module but carried
equivalence kesetaraan Indonesian — vocabulary The state of two algebraic expressions having the same value for all variable values; Indonesian uses 'kesetaraan' or 'e
expand menjabarkan Indonesian — vocabulary To apply the distributive property to remove brackets; Indonesian uses 'menjabarkan'. Out of scope for this module but c
factor memfaktorkan Indonesian — vocabulary To rewrite an expression as a product of its factors; Indonesian uses 'memfaktorkan'. Out of scope for this module but c
degree derajat Indonesian — vocabulary The highest exponent of the variable in a polynomial; Indonesian uses 'derajat', which also means angle degree and acade
Fixed quantity constant — convention Constant '1' in 1 + 3n — fixed quantity from the pattern: Use the fixed-vs-changing table from Exploration 4.1 explicitl
Changing quantity variable term — convention Constant '1' in 1 + 3n — fixed quantity from the pattern: Use the fixed-vs-changing table from Exploration 4.1 explicitl
Research Brief
Learning Objective
Identify a changing quantity in a growing pattern and represent it using a letter (variable), distinguishing it from fixed quantities (constants), and write the algebraic form using the notation 3n.
Misconceptions (3)
#252 — Students believe variables are mysterious unknown numbers to be 'solved for', rather than placeholders that represent any value in a pattern or rule. In this module's context, students see 'n' and immediately try to find its value rather than accepting it as a label for 'any pattern number'. This is the primary target misconception for this module.
Present a completed table (pattern 1→3, 2→6, 3→9) and the expression 3n, then ask: 'What is n?' If the student says 'I need to solve for n' or writes a single number, the misconception is active. The Math_Trap slide should show a student 'solving' 3n = 12 and ask whether this is the right move.
Severity: medium Math_Trap IAS: Counterexample Feedback
#251 — Although the full expression-evaluation error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) belongs to sibling module G7C4M2, a precursor form is relevant here: students may see '1 + 3n' (from Exploration 4.1) and attempt to combine the constant 1 with the variable term 3n into '4n', treating unlike parts as combinable. This is the closure/premature-simplification precursor at the concrete phase.
After deriving 1 + 3n from Nyoman's matchstick pattern, ask: 'Can we simplify 1 + 3n further?' If the student writes 4n, the precursor misconception is active. Use a Counterexample Feedback response: substitute n = 2 to show 1 + 3(2) = 7, not 4(2) = 8.
Severity: high Math_Trap IAS: Counterexample Feedback
#250 — This misconception (guess-and-check as the only equation-solving strategy) is not directly activated by this module's LO, which does not ask students to solve equations. It is listed in the lo_misconceptions_checklist as a linked record but is out of scope for this module — it belongs to the equations strand. Included here for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action needed in this module.
Not applicable at this module scope. This misconception is activated only when students are asked to solve equations (e.g., 3x + 7 = 28), which is not part of this module's LO. Monitor in later equation-focused modules.
Severity: medium none IAS: none
Visual Models (2)
VIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
 — Variable as Pattern Continuation
Primary visual anchor for this module. Ground the variable 'n' as the generalisation of a pattern students already see — Nyoman's matchstick squares grow by 3 each time, so the changing quantity is 3 × (pattern number) = 3n. This directly counters M252
Misconception medium
Students believe variables are mysterious unknown numbers to be 'solved for', rather than placeholders that represent any value in a pattern or rule. In this module's context, students see 'n' and immediately try to find its value rather than accepting it as a label for 'any pattern number'. This is the primary target misconception for this module.
Present a completed table (pattern 1→3, 2→6, 3→9) and the expression 3n, then ask: 'What is n?' If the student says 'I need to solve for n' or writes a single number, the misconception is active. The Math_Trap slide should show a student 'solving' 3n = 12 and ask whether this is the right move.
 (variable as mystery unknown) by showing that n is not a hidden number to find but a label for 'whichever pattern number we are at'. Position at the Snapshot moment immediately after the opening context is established.
concrete introduce_concept moderate
VIR 203
Function Machines: Input → Rule → Output
A function is just a machine: put a number in, apply a rule, get a number out. Every function has this input→process→output structure. The machine metaphor makes functions concrete before formal notation (f(x)) ever appears.
 — Function Machines: Input → Rule → Output
Bridge from the concrete pattern table to the abstract notation 3n. The machine metaphor makes the rule explicit: input = pattern number (n), rule = multiply by 3, output = number of matchsticks added. This positions 3n as a rule-description, not a number to solve. Use after the pattern table is complete and before the Math_Vault formalises the notation.
pictorial bridge_cpa moderate
IAS Patterns (5)
Progressive Reveal
Actions: Attend, Perceive Structure
When: Use to build the matchstick pattern one step at a time — show figure 1, then figure 2, then figure 3 — so students observe the growing quantity before the rule is named. This is the concrete-phase entry into the LO and sets up the Snapshot moment.
Representation Translation
Actions: Compare, Map Representations, Transfer
When: Use to connect the three representations of the same pattern: (1) the visual matchstick figure, (2) the table of fixed vs. changing quantities, and (3) the algebraic form 3n. Students move between all three, confirming that 3n is just a compact way of writing what the table already shows. This is the core CPA bridge for this module.
Counterexample Feedback
Actions: Demonstrate a case that breaks a false rule the learner proposed
When: Deploy in the Math_Trap slide for M252: after a student 'solves for n', show that substituting n = 1, 2, 3 all give valid answers — there is no single correct value of n. Also use for M251 precursor: substitute n = 2 into both 1 + 3n and the incorrect 4n to show they give different answers.
Commit-Before-Reveal
Actions: Predict, Commit
When: Use in the Exit_Question and in the fixed-vs-changing table activity: ask students to predict whether a quantity is fixed or changing before the pattern is extended. Locks in the student's reasoning before the answer is shown, increasing engagement with the variable/constant distinction.
Rule Completion
Actions: Generalize, Reflect
When: Use after the pattern table is complete: present '3 × n = ___' and ask students to write the algebraic form. Then extend to the full expression 1 + 3n. This is the generalisation step that moves from the table to the notation, and is the moment where the Math_Vault definition of 'algebraic form' is anchored.
Convention Flags (4)
Implicit multiplication in 3n
Show 3 × n = 3n side by side in the Math_Vault slide. Use colour-coding: the coefficient 3 in one colour, the variable n in another. Explicitly state: 'In algebra, we drop the × sign when a number multiplies a letter.' Reinforce in every worked example in this module.
Risk: Students from arithmetic backgrounds expect an explicit multiplication sign (3 × n) and may not recognise 3n as '3 times n'. This is the single most critical notation shift in this module and is introduced here for the first time in the chapter.
Variable n as 'any value' vs. 'the unknown value'
Explicitly distinguish the two uses of letters in algebra at the Snapshot moment: 'In a pattern, n tells us which figure we are looking at — it can be 1, 2, 3, or any number. We are not trying to find n; we are using n to describe the rule.' Avoid the word 'unknown' in this module's student-facing content.
Risk: The word 'unknown' appears in the topic's LO slide and summary (from the existing topic content), framing the variable as something 'not known yet' — which primes the M252 misconception. This module's LO frames n as a pattern-position label, not an unknown to solve for. The two framings conflict.
Algebraic form vs. algebraic expression
Treat 'algebraic form' as the primary term for this module (matching the textbook's Exploration 4.1 language) and note in the Math_Vault that 'algebraic expression' means the same thing. Do not introduce 'algebraic expression' as a separate concept here — that disambiguation belongs to G7C4M2.
Risk: The curriculum vocabulary requires introducing 'algebraic form' (bentuk aljabar) as the module's primary term, but the chapter-level vocabulary also includes 'algebraic expression'. These are used interchangeably in the textbook (p.129) but students may treat them as different objects.
Constant '1' in 1 + 3n — fixed quantity from the pattern
Use the fixed-vs-changing table from Exploration 4.1 explicitly: label the column 'Fixed quantity (constant)' and 'Changing quantity (variable term)'. Colour-code the constant 1 and the variable term 3n differently in every representation.
Risk: In Nyoman's matchstick pattern, the constant 1 represents the fixed corner matchstick. Students may not recognise why 1 is a constant (it does not change as n changes) vs. 3n which does change. The structural reason (fixed quantity in the physical pattern) must be made explicit.
Research Gaps (4)
misconception: M250 (guess-and-check equation solving) is linked to this LO in the KB checklist but is not activated by this module's LO. The module does not ask students to solve equations. This is a KB linking error — M250 belongs to the equations strand, not to pattern-to-expression generalisation. No instructional gap exists in this module, but the KB link should be reviewed.
Low for this module. S4 and S8 coverage checks will flag M250 as unaddressed, but this is correct — it is out of scope here. Future equation modules should own M250.
visual_model: No KB VIR exists for a fixed-vs-changing quantity table — the standard concrete-phase scaffold used in Exploration 4.1 (p.127) to distinguish constants from variable quantities before algebraic notation is introduced. VIR 161 (Variable as Pattern Continuation) is the closest but does not include the two-column table structure.
Medium. The fixed-vs-changing table is the primary concrete scaffold for the variable/constant distinction in this module. Blueprint builders must design this without a KB VIR anchor. Recommend KB insertion of a VIR for 'Fixed vs. Changing Quantity Table' at concrete CPA phase.
interactive: No KB IAS entry exists for a 'pattern-to-expression builder' interactive — where students observe a growing visual pattern, complete a table of values, and then drag or type to construct the algebraic form. IAS 61 (Expression Exchange) covers expression building from coins but not from visual growing patterns. This is the most natural interactive for this module's concrete CPA phase.
Medium. The module's primary interactive moment (building 3n from the matchstick pattern) lacks a formally evidenced IAS archetype. The Representation Translation archetype (IAS 4) is the closest available and should be used, but a dedicated pattern-to-expression archetype would strengthen the design.
misconception: The precursor form of M251 active in this module — combining 1 + 3n into 4n (treating the constant and variable term as like terms) — is not explicitly described in the KB misconception record for M251. The KB record describes the full evaluation error (2 + 3x treated as 5x when x = 4), not the simpler 'can I combine 1 and 3n?' question that arises at the concrete phase of this module.
Medium. The Math_Trap for M251 in this module must be designed from first principles rather than from a KB-evidenced surfacing strategy. The blueprint builder should note this as a KB refinement request for M251.
Synthesis Narrative
This module is the chapter's concrete-phase entry point into algebraic thinking, and its central instructional challenge is one of framing: students must encounter the letter 'n' not as a mystery number waiting to be solved, but as a label for 'whichever figure in the pattern we are looking at.' VIR 161 (Variable as Pattern Continuation) is the recommended primary visual anchor precisely because it grounds this reframing in something students already understand — a growing sequence. Nyoman's matchstick square pattern from Exploration 4.1 (p.127) is the ideal concrete context: the pattern grows by 3 matchsticks per figure, the fixed corner matchstick is always 1, and the algebraic form 1 + 3n emerges naturally from the table of fixed vs. changing quantities. The Snapshot trope should fire at the moment the table is complete and the pattern is visible, before any algebraic notation is introduced — this is the perceptual anchor that all subsequent abstraction will reference. The path from the concrete table to the abstract notation 3n runs through two critical instructional moves. First, the fixed-vs-changing distinction must be made explicit and colour-coded: the constant 1 is always the same regardless of which figure we are at, while 3n changes because it depends on n. This distinction is the conceptual foundation for the vocabulary terms 'variable' and 'constant', which the Math_Vault trope should formalise immediately after the table activity. Second, the implicit multiplication convention (3n means 3 × n) must be introduced explicitly — this is the single most important notation shift in the module, and the conventions flag is high-priority. The Representation Translation archetype (IAS 4) is the workhorse here: students move between the visual figure, the table, and the expression 3n, confirming at each step that all three say the same thing. VIR 203 (Function Machines) then bridges to the abstract notation by framing 3n as a rule: input the pattern number, multiply by 3, get the number of matchsticks added. This positions the expression as a description of a process, not a number to solve. The two misconceptions requiring active surfacing in this module are M252 and the precursor form of M251. M252 (variable as mystery unknown) is the higher-priority target: the Math_Trap slide should present a student who, upon seeing 3n, immediately asks 'what is n?' and begins solving. The Counterexample Feedback archetype (IAS 33) is the correct response mechanism — show that n = 1 gives 3, n = 2 gives 6, n = 3 gives 9, and ask: 'Which of these is the right answer for n?' The answer is all of them, depending on which figure we are describing. The M251 precursor (combining 1 + 3n into 4n) should be surfaced in a second Math_Trap: after the full expression 1 + 3n is derived, ask whether it can be simplified further. If a student writes 4n, the Counterexample Feedback fires: substitute n = 2 to show 1 + 3(2) = 7 ≠ 4(2) = 8. M250 (guess-and-check equation solving) is linked in the KB checklist but is not activated by this module's LO and requires no instructional action here. The Exit_Question should use the Commit-Before-Reveal archetype: present a new growing pattern (e.g., a sequence of tile arrangements) and ask students to write the algebraic form, committing before the answer is revealed.
Inheritance Notes
Chapter research confirmed: (1) VIR 161 (Variable as Pattern Continuation) is the recommended concrete-phase entry point for the chapter's variable concept — this module is the first to deploy it. (2) VIR 203 (Function Machines) is confirmed as the CPA bridge from concrete pattern to abstract notation — this module uses it at the pictorial bridge position. (3) The implicit multiplication convention flag (3 × n = 3n) was identified at chapter scope as the highest-priority notation shift for the chapter's first module — confirmed and elevated here. (4) The chapter research flagged the 'variable as fixed unknown' misconception as having no KB record — this module's M252 is the closest KB-linked misconception and is the primary target. (5) The fixed-vs-changing quantity table gap (no KB VIR) was identified at chapter scope — confirmed as a design gap for this module's blueprint. (6) All translation notes from chapter research are carried forward in full.
Ideal Blueprint
Blueprint LO
Identify a changing quantity in a growing pattern and represent it using a letter (variable), distinguishing it from fixed quantities (constants), and write the algebraic form using the notation 3n. Learn
CPA Map
C Slides 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13
P Slides 7
A Slides 14, 15, 16, 17
This is a concrete-phase Learn module. The progression runs: Concrete observation (Slides 1–4) → Concrete formalisation with vocabulary (Slides 5–6) → Pictorial/Transition bridge via Function Machine (Slide 7) → Concrete derivation of full expression (Slides 8–9) → Concrete interactive practice (Slides 10–11) → Concrete misconception confrontation (Slides 12–13) → Abstract choral recall and summary (Slides 14–15) → Abstract transfer and exit (Slides 16–17). The module does not reach full Abstract phase — it ends at the boundary of concrete-to-abstract, consistent with the primary CPA phase designation of 'concrete'. Abstract slides (14–17) are summary and exit only, not new abstract instruction.
Slide Sequence (17 slides)
1
Activate — Motivational hook — surface prior knowledge of growing patterns from arithmetic; establish that some quantities change and some stay fixed.
Show a real-world Indonesian context: a row of warungs (food stalls) at a market, each stall needing a fixed number of poles plus extra ropes per stall. Ask: 'What stays the same as we add more stalls? What changes?' Students discuss in pairs. No algebra yet — purely observational. Teacher facilitates a brief share-out. This activates the fixed-vs-changing distinction from everyday experience before the matchstick pattern formalises it.
Concrete Snapshot
2
Activate — Prerequisite check — confirm students can extend a simple numeric sequence and describe the rule in words before algebraic notation is introduced.
Display three simple sequences (e.g., 3, 6, 9, 12, … and 5, 8, 11, 14, …). Ask students to: (1) write the next two terms, (2) describe the rule in words ('add 3 each time'). Teacher collects responses to gauge readiness. No letters or algebra yet. This confirms the prerequisite of pattern-rule description in words, which the Teach section will then translate into algebraic form.
Concrete
3
Teach — Concrete introduction — present Nyoman's matchstick square pattern (Exploration 4.1, p.127) one figure at a time using Progressive Reveal; students observe the growing quantity before any rule is named.
Progressive Reveal: show Figure 1 (1 square = 4 matchsticks), then Figure 2 (2 squares = 7 matchsticks), then Figure 3 (3 squares = 10 matchsticks). Each reveal is animated — matchsticks appear one group at a time. Ask after each reveal: 'How many matchsticks now? What changed? What stayed the same?' Students record observations. The fixed corner matchstick (always 1) and the growing group (3 per new square) become visible through observation, not instruction. This is the concrete-phase entry into the LO and sets up the Snapshot moment.
Concrete SnapshotVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
4
Teach — Concrete table activity — students complete the fixed-vs-changing quantity table from Exploration 4.1 (p.127), making the variable/constant distinction explicit before any letter is introduced.
Display a two-column table: 'Fixed quantity' | 'Changing quantity'. Rows for Figure 1, 2, 3, and a blank row for Figure 4. Students fill in: Fixed = 1 (the corner matchstick, always the same); Changing = 3, 6, 9, 12 (the matchsticks added per square). Colour-coding: fixed quantity column in orange, changing quantity column in blue — this colour scheme is maintained throughout the module. Teacher asks: 'Why is 1 always in the fixed column?' and 'Why does the changing column grow by 3 each time?' This is the primary concrete scaffold for the variable/constant distinction. Note: no KB VIR exists for this table structure (gap identified in research brief); the table is designed from first principles with colour-coding as the visual anchor.
Concrete
5
Teach — Introduce the letter n as a label for the pattern number — the critical reframing from 'unknown to solve' to 'position label'. Math_Vault for variable and constant.
Teacher says: 'Instead of writing Figure 1, Figure 2, Figure 3 every time, mathematicians use a letter. We write n to mean: whichever figure number we are looking at.' Show the table again with a new column: 'Figure number = n'. Emphasise: 'We are NOT trying to find n. n is just our label for the position in the pattern — it can be 1, 2, 3, or any number.' Explicitly avoid the word 'unknown'. Then introduce the Math_Vault: VARIABLE — a letter that represents a changing quantity. In this pattern, n is the variable because the figure number changes. CONSTANT — a number that stays the same. In this pattern, 1 is the constant because the fixed corner matchstick never changes. ALGEBRAIC FORM — a mathematical expression that uses letters and numbers together to describe a rule. Note: 'algebraic expression' means the same thing; we use 'algebraic form' in this module. Colour-code: n in blue (changing), 1 in orange (fixed).
Concrete Math_VaultVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
6
Teach — Introduce the notation 3n — the implicit multiplication convention. Math_Vault for 3n notation.
Return to the changing-quantity column: 3, 6, 9, 12. Ask: 'How do we get each of these from the figure number n?' Students should see: 3 × 1 = 3, 3 × 2 = 6, 3 × 3 = 9. Write: 'Changing quantity = 3 × n'. Then introduce the convention: 'In algebra, when a number multiplies a letter, we drop the × sign. So 3 × n is written as 3n.' Show side by side: 3 × n = 3n. Colour-code: 3 in one colour (the multiplier), n in blue (the variable). Math_Vault entry: '3n means 3 × n. In algebra, we omit the × sign when a number multiplies a letter. This is the standard algebraic convention.' Reinforce: 'So the changing quantity in Nyoman's pattern is 3n — three times whichever figure number we are at.'
Concrete Math_VaultVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
7
Teach — Pictorial bridge — Function Machine visual connects the concrete table to the abstract notation 3n, framing the expression as a rule (input → process → output) rather than a number to solve.
Display VIR 203
Function Machines: Input → Rule → Output
A function is just a machine: put a number in, apply a rule, get a number out. Every function has this input→process→output structure. The machine metaphor makes functions concrete before formal notation (f(x)) ever appears.
 (Function Machine): Input box labelled 'Pattern number (n)', arrow into a machine labelled '× 3', output box labelled 'Matchsticks added'. Show three passes: n = 1 → 3, n = 2 → 6, n = 3 → 9. Ask: 'What does the machine do to any number we put in?' Students articulate: 'It multiplies by 3.' Teacher: 'So the rule is 3n — multiply the pattern number by 3. n is the input; 3n is the output. We are not solving for n; we are describing what happens to any n.' This positions 3n as a rule-description, not a number to find. Bridges from the concrete table (Slide 4) to the abstract notation (Slide 6).
Transition VIR 203
Function Machines: Input → Rule → Output
A function is just a machine: put a number in, apply a rule, get a number out. Every function has this input→process→output structure. The machine metaphor makes functions concrete before formal notation (f(x)) ever appears.
8
Teach — Derive the full algebraic form 1 + 3n from the pattern — combining the constant and the variable term into a single expression.
Return to the matchstick figures. Ask: 'The changing quantity is 3n. But we also have the fixed corner matchstick. How many matchsticks in total for any figure n?' Students should combine: Total = 1 (fixed) + 3n (changing) = 1 + 3n. Write this step by step: Total matchsticks = fixed quantity + changing quantity = 1 + 3n. Verify with the table: n = 1 → 1 + 3(1) = 4 ✓; n = 2 → 1 + 3(2) = 7 ✓; n = 3 → 1 + 3(3) = 10 ✓. Colour-code: 1 in orange (constant), 3n in blue (variable term). State: 'The algebraic form for Nyoman's matchstick pattern is 1 + 3n.' This directly enacts the module LO and uses Exploration 4.1 (p.127) as-is.
Concrete VIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
9
Teach — Formalise the anatomy of 1 + 3n using p.129 Section A key example — identify n as variable, 1 as constant, and 3n as the variable term. Snapshot trope.
Display the expression 1 + 3n with colour-coded labels (from p.129 Section A key example, used as-is). Label: '1' → constant (orange); 'n' → variable (blue); '3n' → the part that changes (blue). Explicitly note: '3 is the multiplier of n' — but do NOT introduce the word 'coefficient' here (that vocabulary belongs to G7C4M2). State: 'The algebraic form 1 + 3n has two parts: the constant 1, and the variable part 3n.' Snapshot trope: this is the durable visual anchor — the labelled expression 1 + 3n with colour-coded constant and variable part — that all subsequent practice will reference. Students copy this into their notebooks as the module's key image.
Concrete SnapshotVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
10
Get — Guided worked example — Representation Translation: students move between the visual figure, the table, and the expression 1 + 3n, confirming all three say the same thing.
Applet placeholder: 'Representation Translation Applet — Matchstick Pattern Explorer' (see Applet 1). Before the applet: teacher sets context — 'You are going to move between three views of the same pattern: the picture, the table, and the algebraic form. Your job is to confirm they all match.' Watch-for prompt: 'Notice how the number in the table and the result from 1 + 3n are always the same.' Students interact with the applet to complete the Representation Translation activity. This is the core CPA bridge for the module.
Concrete VIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
Applet: Applet 1: Representation Translation — Matchstick Pattern Explorer
11
Get — Post-applet consolidation — Snapshot reconnects the Representation Translation activity to the module LO.
Display the three representations side by side: (1) the matchstick figure for n = 4, (2) the completed table row (n = 4, fixed = 1, changing = 12, total = 13), (3) the expression 1 + 3(4) = 13. Snapshot trope: 'All three say the same thing. The algebraic form 1 + 3n is a compact rule that works for any figure number n.' Teacher facilitates brief discussion: 'Which representation is easiest to use for large n? Why?' This consolidates the CPA bridge and reinforces the power of algebraic form over listing.
Concrete SnapshotVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
12
Ask — Math_Trap — surface and confront M252
Misconception medium
Students believe variables are mysterious unknown numbers to be 'solved for', rather than placeholders that represent any value in a pattern or rule. In this module's context, students see 'n' and immediately try to find its value rather than accepting it as a label for 'any pattern number'. This is the primary target misconception for this module.
Present a completed table (pattern 1→3, 2→6, 3→9) and the expression 3n, then ask: 'What is n?' If the student says 'I need to solve for n' or writes a single number, the misconception is active. The Math_Trap slide should show a student 'solving' 3n = 12 and ask whether this is the right move.
: the misconception that n is a mystery unknown to be solved for.
Math_Trap for M252
Misconception medium
Students believe variables are mysterious unknown numbers to be 'solved for', rather than placeholders that represent any value in a pattern or rule. In this module's context, students see 'n' and immediately try to find its value rather than accepting it as a label for 'any pattern number'. This is the primary target misconception for this module.
Present a completed table (pattern 1→3, 2→6, 3→9) and the expression 3n, then ask: 'What is n?' If the student says 'I need to solve for n' or writes a single number, the misconception is active. The Math_Trap slide should show a student 'solving' 3n = 12 and ask whether this is the right move.
. Display a speech bubble from a student character named Budi: 'I see 3n. I need to find what n is. Let me solve: 3n = 12, so n = 4.' Ask the class: 'Is Budi right? Is n = 4 the only answer?' Counterexample Feedback: show the table — n = 1 gives 3, n = 2 gives 6, n = 3 gives 9, n = 4 gives 12. Ask: 'Which of these is the right value of n?' Answer: all of them — n is the pattern number, and it can be 1, 2, 3, 4, or any figure number. The trap: Budi found one value of n that makes 3n = 12, but n is not a fixed unknown — it is a label for any position in the pattern. Correct framing: 'We are not solving for n. We are using n to describe the rule for any figure.' Colour-code n in blue throughout. Reference VIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
.
Concrete Math_TrapVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
13
Ask — Math_Trap — surface and confront the M251
Misconception high
Although the full expression-evaluation error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) belongs to sibling module G7C4M2, a precursor form is relevant here: students may see '1 + 3n' (from Exploration 4.1) and attempt to combine the constant 1 with the variable term 3n into '4n', treating unlike parts as combinable. This is the closure/premature-simplification precursor at the concrete phase.
After deriving 1 + 3n from Nyoman's matchstick pattern, ask: 'Can we simplify 1 + 3n further?' If the student writes 4n, the precursor misconception is active. Use a Counterexample Feedback response: substitute n = 2 to show 1 + 3(2) = 7, not 4(2) = 8.
 precursor: combining 1 + 3n into 4n (treating unlike parts as combinable).
Math_Trap for M251
Misconception high
Although the full expression-evaluation error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) belongs to sibling module G7C4M2, a precursor form is relevant here: students may see '1 + 3n' (from Exploration 4.1) and attempt to combine the constant 1 with the variable term 3n into '4n', treating unlike parts as combinable. This is the closure/premature-simplification precursor at the concrete phase.
After deriving 1 + 3n from Nyoman's matchstick pattern, ask: 'Can we simplify 1 + 3n further?' If the student writes 4n, the precursor misconception is active. Use a Counterexample Feedback response: substitute n = 2 to show 1 + 3(2) = 7, not 4(2) = 8.
 precursor. Display a speech bubble from a student character named Sari: 'I can simplify 1 + 3n. Since 1 + 3 = 4, the answer is 4n.' Ask: 'Is Sari right? Can we combine 1 and 3n?' Counterexample Feedback: substitute n = 2. Sari's version: 4(2) = 8. Correct version: 1 + 3(2) = 1 + 6 = 7. These are different! So 1 + 3n ≠ 4n. Explain: '1 is a constant — it does not have n. 3n is a variable part — it has n. They are different kinds of quantities and cannot be combined.' Colour-code: 1 in orange (constant), 3n in blue (variable part). Reinforce: 'The algebraic form 1 + 3n is already in its simplest form.' Note: this is the precursor form of M251
Misconception high
Although the full expression-evaluation error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) belongs to sibling module G7C4M2, a precursor form is relevant here: students may see '1 + 3n' (from Exploration 4.1) and attempt to combine the constant 1 with the variable term 3n into '4n', treating unlike parts as combinable. This is the closure/premature-simplification precursor at the concrete phase.
After deriving 1 + 3n from Nyoman's matchstick pattern, ask: 'Can we simplify 1 + 3n further?' If the student writes 4n, the precursor misconception is active. Use a Counterexample Feedback response: substitute n = 2 to show 1 + 3(2) = 7, not 4(2) = 8.
 at the concrete phase; the full M251
Misconception high
Although the full expression-evaluation error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) belongs to sibling module G7C4M2, a precursor form is relevant here: students may see '1 + 3n' (from Exploration 4.1) and attempt to combine the constant 1 with the variable term 3n into '4n', treating unlike parts as combinable. This is the closure/premature-simplification precursor at the concrete phase.
After deriving 1 + 3n from Nyoman's matchstick pattern, ask: 'Can we simplify 1 + 3n further?' If the student writes 4n, the precursor misconception is active. Use a Counterexample Feedback response: substitute n = 2 to show 1 + 3(2) = 7, not 4(2) = 8.
 (evaluation error) belongs to G7C4M2.
Concrete Math_TrapVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
14
Review — Shout_Out_Loud — whole-class choral active recall of the three vocabulary terms and the notation 3n.
Shout_Out_Loud trope. Teacher displays three prompts one at a time, class responds chorally: (1) 'A letter that represents a changing quantity is called a…' → VARIABLE. (2) 'A number that stays the same in an expression is called a…' → CONSTANT. (3) 'A mathematical expression using letters and numbers to describe a rule is called an…' → ALGEBRAIC FORM. (4) '3 × n written without the multiplication sign is…' → 3n. After each response, teacher confirms and shows the colour-coded term on screen. This is the Review section's active recall moment — high energy, whole class, no writing required.
Abstract Shout_Out_Loud
15
Review — Snapshot summary — the module's key learning in one durable image: the labelled algebraic form 1 + 3n with all three vocabulary terms anchored.
Display the module's canonical summary image: the expression 1 + 3n with three labelled callouts — '1: constant (fixed quantity, does not change)', 'n: variable (changing quantity, the pattern number)', '3n: variable part (3 × n, changes with n)'. Below: 'Algebraic form: 1 + 3n'. Also show: '3 × n = 3n (we drop the × sign in algebra).' This is the Snapshot trope for the Review section — the single image students should be able to reconstruct from memory. Students copy into notebooks if not already done.
Abstract SnapshotVIR 161
Variable as Pattern Continuation
A variable isn't a mysterious 'x' to solve for — it's the rule that describes a pattern. If the pattern is 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., the variable expression is 2n. Students discover variables by generalizing patterns they already see.
16
Consolidate — Real-world grounding — Math_World: connect algebraic form to an authentic Indonesian context beyond matchsticks, showing that the same structure (constant + variable part) appears in everyday life.
Math_World trope. Present a new Indonesian context: a batik workshop in Yogyakarta where each artisan needs 1 fixed tool (a canting) plus 4 wax blocks per pattern they make. Ask: 'If an artisan makes n patterns, how many wax blocks do they need in total?' Students identify: fixed quantity = 1 (the canting), changing quantity = 4n (wax blocks). Algebraic form: 1 + 4n. Teacher connects: 'This is the same structure as Nyoman's matchstick pattern — a constant plus a variable part. Algebraic form lets us describe any growing situation with a simple rule.' This grounds the module LO in a culturally authentic Indonesian context and shows transfer beyond the textbook example.
Abstract
17
Consolidate — Exit_Question — Commit-Before-Reveal: students write the algebraic form for a new growing pattern, committing before the answer is revealed. Diagnostic check of module LO.
Exit_Question trope. Display a new growing pattern: a sequence of tile arrangements where each figure has 2 fixed corner tiles plus 5 tiles added per figure (so Figure 1 = 7, Figure 2 = 12, Figure 3 = 17). Show the first three figures visually. Ask: (1) 'What is the fixed quantity?' (2) 'What is the changing quantity?' (3) 'Write the algebraic form for the total number of tiles in Figure n.' Students write their answers independently (Commit). Then reveal: fixed = 2 (constant), changing = 5n (variable part), algebraic form = 2 + 5n. Verify: n = 1 → 2 + 5 = 7 ✓; n = 2 → 2 + 10 = 12 ✓. Commit-Before-Reveal archetype: the student's written commitment before the reveal is the diagnostic evidence. Teacher collects or photographs responses for formative assessment.
Abstract Exit_Question
Applet Placements (1)
Applet 1: Representation Translation — Matchstick Pattern Explorer — after slide 9
LO: Identify a changing quantity in a growing pattern and represent it using a letter (variable), distinguishing it from fixed quantities (constants), and write the algebraic form using the notation 3n.
Explore IAS: Representation Translation Misconception: M252 — variable as mystery unknown; M251 precursor — combining unlike parts
Visual Models (2)
VIR 161 — Variable as Pattern Continuation
Concrete At slide 3 high
VIR 203 — Function Machines: Input → Rule → Output
Pictorial At slide 7 medium
Trope Map
Math Vault
Positions: 5, 6
Snapshots
Positions: 1, 3, 9, 11, 15
Math Traps
Positions: 12, 13
Exit Question
Positions: 17

How to read this review

A review is the scorer's diagnosis of a single module (or applet). The per-slide grid at the bottom is the main working area; the sections above it are summaries and context.

Header & scores

  • Verdict (ship / upgrade / rework / fail) and overall score summarise the compiled review.
  • Mastery coverage is the S8 estimate of how much of the LO the module actually delivers.
  • Reviewer directives banner (amber, near the top) lists any approved-gate directives that flowed into the scorers this run.
  • Quadrant strip shows P / D / X / L percent scores with S9 blurbs.

Vocabulary Registry

A module-level table of every term this module is responsible for: A1's planned terms (with authoritative definitions), KB-supplementary terms, and scorer-discovered terms. Each row shows delivery status (established / mentioned / not introduced) and source. This is the one-glance answer to "what vocabulary does this module own?"

What's Working Well

Positive observations surfaced from scorer reasoning — criteria that scored at max, grouped by quadrant. The full reasoning text is included so you can see what the scorer thought was strong and why. Previously these stayed buried inside the collapsed Scorer details; this card pulls them up front.

Gates & Antipatterns

Module-level scoring signals. Gates are hard/critical pass/fail checks; antipatterns are structural issues found by S7. Click any failed gate or antipattern id for a description in the modal.

Slide Review (per-slide grid)

Each slide row has the thumbnail, the scorer-synthesised slide action wrapped in a priority chip (see symbol legend below), and a collapsed "Scorer details" block with the full criterion annotations. Below that sits the enrichment strip — each row is a distinct commitment the blueprint or scorer anchored to this slide:

  • Concept — what the reviewer actually saw (or thinks should be added) on this slide. Chips are tagged by action: introduces (green — slide establishes the concept for the first time in the module), reinforces / practices (blue — slide revisits a concept established earlier), add here (red dashed — reviewer recommends the slide deliver this concept but it currently does not), reinforce harder (amber dashed — slide mentions the concept but the reviewer recommends exercising it harder). Each chip carries a strength tag (explicit / partial / weak / missing) and an evidence snippet in the tooltip. Sourced from the S6 scorer's concepts_on_slide array (P and D quadrants) — what the reviewer observed, not what the blueprint planned.
  • Vocab introduced — A1-planned terms the module introduces at this slide, from blueprint.vocabulary_introduction_plan[].introduced_at_slide. Chip tooltip shows the authoritative definition.
  • Vocab in screen (applet reviews only) — terms the screen exercises. Sub-tag reinforced means the screen actively practices the term (drag / tap / input / generate); used means the term only appears passively.
  • Misc addressed — misconception ids the slide explicitly targets, with effectiveness 0/3. Primary source is blueprint.slides[].misconceptions_targeted (plan); the scorer's evidence_slides feeds the effectiveness score. Click the chip for the full how-addressed narrative.
  • Visual model — VIR id + title + CPA phase used on this slide. Click to open the VIR's full SVG preview and KB metadata in a modal.
  • Prerequisites — prior-module / prior-chapter concepts this slide reactivates. Populated on Activate-section bridges. From blueprint.slides[].prerequisites_activated.
  • Convention — false-friend or convention requirements the slide must satisfy. From blueprint.convention_requirements[].addressed_at_slide.
  • Textbook — textbook pages the slide uses, with usage tag (use_as_is / adapted / supplemented / referenced). From blueprint.textbook_alignment[].slide_positions.
  • Exit — marker on the slide that is the module's exit-verification question (from blueprint.trope_map.exit_question).
  • Flagged — criterion ids the scorer rated below max on this slide. Use this to jump directly to the problem spots.
  • Stage (applet reviews only) — 1-6 stage tag + stage name + cognitive action + interaction mode + scaffolding level + CPA phase, derived from the applet blueprint screen that this PPTX slide belongs to.

Click any chip with a tooltip indicator to open its full detail in a modal.

Priority & operation symbols

The Path to 100 section and each slide's action card use pill chips instead of inline text. Scan by glyph:

  • ⊘ Must fix — gate or antipattern requires this before ship
  • ↑ Should fix — quality upgrade recommended
  • ◦ Consider — nice-to-have, not blocking

Operation chips next to each action name the recommendation verb from the recommendation protocol:

  • ✖delete remove slide / content
  • +insert add new slide / element
  • ✎edit modify existing content
  • ↵merge combine two into one
  • ✂split one slide into several
  • ⇄relocate move to different position — notably for worked-example → applet conversion

Mastery Assessment

S8's narrative assessment: coverage percent with notes, what students CAN do after the module, fluency gaps, conceptual depth, transfer readiness, and the mastery path to 100. Per-misconception detail now lives in each slide's enrichment strip instead of a separate list here.

Path to 100

Priority-ordered upgrade actions from the scorer's upgrade_map, grouped as:

  • Gate recommendations — must-fix items from failed hard/critical gates or antipatterns. Lead with a priority chip + operation chip + source label.
  • Priority actions — per-criterion upgrades sorted by gap magnitude. Priority is derived from the gap (gap ≥ 3 → must-fix, ≥ 2 → should-fix, otherwise consider) when the scorer didn't emit one explicitly. Each row links back to the criterion's annotation anchor in the slide grid.
  • Mastery gaps — narrative gaps from S8 that don't have a specific criterion owner.

Research brief & Blueprint overlays

The linked Research brief and Blueprint overlays contain the upstream inputs the scorers used — misconceptions, VIRs, IAS patterns, slide sequence, vocabulary introduction plan, textbook alignment. Open them from the overlay buttons to cross-reference with the review.
